note: All 45 answers (15 per company) for Mario Lizarraga. Humanized, STAR-structured.

meta.0:
So at Safran, we had this robot arm test system I'd built — a UFactory 850 with a FUTEK load cell — and it started failing intermittently. About 15% of panels were randomly failing when they shouldn't have. Production was backing up.

[TASK] I needed to figure out the root cause fast because every false failure meant retesting manually.

[ACTION] Instead of just recalibrating like everyone suggested, I pulled two weeks of force data and analyzed it in Python with SciPy. I plotted every press cycle color-coded by pass/fail, and a pattern jumped out — failures correlated with specific fixture positions, not random panels. Mechanical, not electrical.

Inspected the fixture and found micro-cracks in the 3D-printed end-effector. After thousands of cycles, it was shifting about 0.3mm under load. Redesigned it in SolidWorks with beefier geometry and switched from FDM to SLA for better material properties.

[RESULT] False failures dropped to under 1%. Added fixture inspections to our PM schedule every 2,000 cycles. Three days of investigation saved us months of chasing a ghost calibration problem.
meta.1:
When I started building the robot arm test system, I had a choice — spend six months making it perfect, or ship something that works in six weeks.

[SITUATION] The legacy equipment was crashing constantly, costing us three-plus days of downtime monthly. We couldn't wait.

[ACTION] I shipped the 80% version first. Handled the most common panel types automatically, remaining 20% still needed manual loading. Not elegant, but it worked. Then I kept iterating — fixture adapters, software improvements, more panel types automated over the following months.

[RESULT] We went from 30 min/panel to 10 min within six weeks. The remaining 20% got automated the following quarter. Lesson: don't let perfect kill good, especially when people are losing hours daily.
meta.2:
Sure. I start with the fundamentals before touching any hardware.

First — what are we testing? Pass/fail criteria? Expected volume? Once that's clear: define test specs, design the fixture interface, pick instrumentation — force sensors, position sensors, cameras, whatever the test needs — then design the data pipeline. Sampling rates, filtering, storage.

Then control software — test sequence, safety interlocks, error handling. Build a prototype, validate with known-good and known-bad parts. Write procedures, train operators, deploy with monitoring to track yield.

That's exactly what I did at Safran — UFactory arm, FUTEK load cell, STM32 continuity board, Python pipeline. The fixture design alone went through four-plus revisions before we nailed sub-millimeter alignment.
meta.3:
My manager at Safran didn't want to build the robot arm test system. He felt the legacy machines were fine — why spend money?

[TASK] I disagreed but knew just arguing wouldn't work. I needed numbers.

[ACTION] Put together a PowerPoint backed by Excel calculations with real production data. Three key points: legacy machines were single points of failure — crashes stopped production for days. Each crash meant replacing a $750-plus force sensor, happening quarterly. And the UFactory 850 was around $15K total, recoverable in sensor savings alone.

What really got him was the electrical continuity testing angle. Technicians were doing that manually and we'd been having test escapes — bad panels slipping through. The robot would catch every one.

[RESULT] He approved it. Once it was running six months and matching projections, he became one of its biggest supporters. Uses it as an example of automation wins in department reviews.
meta.4:
I've worked with a few types. Main one is FUTEK load cells for force measurement — capturing force-vs-displacement curves during robotic press cycles. Built a Python pipeline with SciPy: Savitzky-Golay smoothing, peak detection, travel measurement, inflection point identification.

Also thermocouples — MAX31850K via 1-Wire on Raspberry Pi 5 — for our paint cure monitoring. Samples oven temps every five seconds with WiFi offline buffering to MariaDB.

And accelerometers on test fixtures for vibration characterization. Biggest challenge across all of them is noise — industrial environments are electrically noisy. Median filters and multi-scale smoothing are my go-to.
meta.5:
Biggest impact — hands down — is the robot arm test system.

[SITUATION] Before I arrived, technicians tested each panel completely by hand. Press every switch, check force and continuity, write down results. Thirty minutes per panel.

[ACTION] I designed and built an automated system: UFactory arm presses switches, FUTEK measures force curves, STM32 checks continuity simultaneously. Everything logged automatically.

[RESULT] Test time dropped to 10 minutes. At 75 panels per week, that's 25-plus hours freed weekly — over 1,300 hours per year. I can measure it directly because the system logs every cycle. Real data, not estimates.
meta.6:
I break it into six steps.

First, define requirements — what does 'qualified' mean? Performance specs, safety, reliability targets like MTBF, environmental conditions.

Second, risk analysis — what can go wrong? Mechanical, electrical, software, operator error. Each risk gets a mitigation.

Third, test plan — functional tests, stress tests, endurance tests (10,000-plus cycles), safety tests (does it fail safely?).

Fourth, execute with known-good and known-bad samples. Document everything.

Fifth, acceptance criteria review — pass everything? Conditional passes? Waivers?

Sixth, deploy — install, train operators, maintenance schedule, monitoring.

At Safran I ran our robot arm through this whole process. Endurance testing was where we caught the fixture degradation issue that led to the SLA material redesign.
meta.7:
So for the robot arm system I needed a custom continuity tester. Nothing off the shelf did what I needed.

[SITUATION] I'd never written C firmware for STM32 before. My background was Python and some C#.

[ACTION] Gave myself a week. Started with STM32CubeIDE tutorials, found example projects for serial comms and GPIO. Three days in I had a working prototype reading pin states over UART. By end of week — debounce logic, multi-channel scanning, error handling.

[RESULT] That firmware is in production now, running on every test cycle. What made it work was focusing on exactly what I needed — serial comms and GPIO reads — not trying to learn all of embedded programming at once.
meta.8:
I try to prioritize based on impact and urgency, not who's asking loudest.

[SITUATION] At Safran I was juggling the robot arm system, the monitoring platform, the Work Order Tracker, and supporting five interns — all simultaneously.

[ACTION] I set up a simple rule: production-blocking issues first, then projects that free the most time for others, then my own development work. I also time-boxed the intern support — three meetings per week, structured sprints — so mentoring didn't eat my whole day.

When my manager wanted a new feature on the monitoring platform while I was debugging a robot arm issue, I showed him the downtime data — the robot fix would save more production hours. He agreed to wait a week on the feature.

[RESULT] Everything shipped, just not all at once. The key is being transparent about trade-offs — don't just say 'I'm busy,' show the data on what each project is worth.
meta.9:
Our robot arm started flagging panels that shouldn't have been failing. Technicians wanted me to recalibrate.

[ACTION] Instead of recalibrating blindly, I pulled two weeks of raw data into Python. Plotted every cycle color-coded by pass/fail, time of day, panel type, fixture position. Clear pattern — failures spiked in the afternoon.

Cross-referenced with temperature logs. The 3D-printed fixture was thermally expanding — afternoon sun heats that part of the shop floor, shifting alignment about 0.3mm.

[RESULT] Root cause was material choice, not calibration. Switched to a low-thermal-expansion material. False failures: 15% down to under 1%. If I'd just recalibrated like everyone wanted, we'd still be chasing this problem.
meta.10:
The monitoring platform, honestly. Before I built it, there was no system tracking what was happening with our equipment — downtime, failures, trends. Just a notebook.

[ACTION] I saw the gap and built a complete platform from scratch — Python backend, SQL database, REST APIs, web dashboards, automated alerts. Nobody asked me to do it. I designed it to replace the notebook with something that could actually analyze patterns.

[RESULT] Unplanned downtime went from three days per month to 30 minutes every six months. That was innovative not because the technology was cutting-edge, but because nobody had thought to apply software engineering to a manual manufacturing environment. The innovation was asking 'why are we tracking this in a notebook in 2024?'
meta.11:
I think about it as making the next person's job easier. If I get hit by a bus, can someone else run my systems?

[ACTION] For the robot arm test system, I wrote seven-plus service documents: setup procedures, calibration guides, troubleshooting decision trees, known failure modes with fixes, and maintenance schedules. Each document targets a specific audience — technicians get step-by-step with photos, engineers get the architecture and theory.

[RESULT] When I onboarded the five interns, the documentation let them operate and maintain the system independently within a week. That's the test — if a new person can get productive in a week using just your docs, you've documented it well enough.
meta.12:
Supervised five interns over about two years at Safran, all on AI defect detection and robotics.

[ACTION] Set up two-week sprints with clear milestones. Gave each intern ownership of a defined scope. Met three times a week — didn't hand them answers, taught them to break problems down.

Matched projects to skill levels — stronger programmers got the ML model work, the mechanically-oriented intern did camera mounting and fixture design.

[RESULT] They delivered a 92% accuracy classification model for visual defect detection, plus a working hardware prototype. One intern's work became the foundation for the vision inspection gantry project. I'm proud of that — they did real engineering, not busy work.
meta.13:
I think about where I can have the most impact. In five years I'd like to be leading an automation or robotics program — not just building one system, but designing the strategy for how systems get deployed across an organization.

At Safran I've gone from building one robot arm to building monitoring platforms, Work Order Trackers, vision inspection systems. The natural next step is doing that at a larger scale — more systems, more facilities, bigger problems. I want to be the person who looks at an operation and says 'here's how we automate this end to end' and then actually makes it happen.
meta.14:
[FOR FIELD SERVICE ENGINEER] Honestly? I want to work on robotics at a scale that matters. At Safran I built one system and own every piece of it. Meta is deploying electro-mechanical systems across facilities worldwide. I want that full-lifecycle ownership I've developed — design through field support — applied where the fleet is hundreds of systems, not one. Plus I live in Redmond and genuinely enjoy being on-site solving problems.

[FOR RESEARCH ENGINEER, ROBOTICS] What draws me is the prototyping and embedded systems combination. At Safran I designed a robotic test platform from scratch — fixtures, STM32 firmware, Python pipelines, the whole stack. Meta's robotics team is building custom machines at a complexity that would push me technically. When I saw this role values aerospace background and automation equipment design — that's literally my profile.

[GENERAL] Three things. Technical challenge — Meta operates at a scale very few companies do. Culture — 'Move Fast' and 'Focus on Impact' are how I naturally work. I shipped a working system in weeks, not months. And location — I live five minutes from the Redmond office.

microsoft.0:
Good failure story. My boss and I built this Work Order Tracker at Safran — Arduino devices with WiFi, touchscreens, barcode scanners in 3D-printed cases, deployed building-wide. TV dashboards showing what's in each rack.

[SITUATION] We made several mistakes.

[ACTION] First — way too ambitious. Launched every feature at once, overwhelmed people. Many forgot to scan, so data was garbage. Second, hardware problems we didn't anticipate: spotty WiFi, 3D cases breaking from being knocked around, screens going white randomly. Third — and this still bugs me — I chose Arduinos without thinking about maintainability. Eighty-plus devices, and every code update means walking around with a laptop.

[WHAT I LEARNED] Stripped it to basics — scan in, scan out, show your rack. Once comfortable, slowly rolled out features. IT set up dedicated WiFi. Redesigned cases. If I did it again: OTA-capable hardware from day one, pilot with one department before going building-wide. System works great now — over a year since the last update — but the rollout was way rougher than necessary.
microsoft.1:
I'd structure it in layers, protocol level up.

First — protocol validation. Controllers talking correctly? Serial, I2C, Modbus — loopback tests, packet sniffers, verify handshake.

Second — functional testing. Each control loop working? Sensors reading correctly, actuators responding, safety interlocks triggering.

Third — stress testing. Push to extremes. Max load, rapid cycling, inject network latency, simulate power fluctuations.

Fourth — failure mode testing. Critical one. Kill a sensor — what happens? Drop communication — does it fail safely? You want to know failure behavior before it happens in the field.

Fifth — environmental. Temperature cycling, vibration, humidity if applicable.

Sixth — full integration. Everything running simultaneously, because individual-pass systems can still fail integrated.

At Safran I did this for the robot arm control system — validated FUTEK readings, STM32 comms, and robot movements all together under production conditions.
microsoft.2:
My manager at Safran didn't want to invest in the robot arm. Legacy equipment was 'good enough.'

[TASK] No budget authority. Had to influence through data.

[ACTION] Built a formal presentation — PowerPoint, Excel-backed calculations, real test data. Showed quarterly $750-plus sensor replacements from crashes, three days monthly downtime, ROI projection of 1,300-plus hours per year freed.

Turning point was showing the robot could automate electrical continuity testing too — technicians did that manually with test escapes happening. Not just faster, but more reliable.

[RESULT] He approved it and became one of its strongest advocates once results matched projections. Six months in production. Lesson: when you can't use authority, use data and demonstrated value.
microsoft.3:
At Safran, I proposed using 3D-printed fixtures for the robot arm. A colleague in manufacturing wanted machined aluminum — more durable, proven.

[ACTION] Instead of just arguing my preference, I set up an actual comparison. Printed one fixture, machined one, ran both through 1,000 test cycles logging dimensional accuracy.

Data showed SLA-printed fixtures held tolerance for about 2,000 cycles before degrading. Aluminum was permanent but cost 10x more with a three-week lead time.

[RESULT] Proposed a hybrid — SLA for development and iteration, aluminum for the final production design once it stabilizes. Colleague agreed because the data made the trade-off clear. Ended up going through four-plus revisions — if we'd machined each one, it would've taken months instead of days.
microsoft.4:
Sure. At Safran I built a Python pipeline using SciPy for sensor data analysis — FUTEK load cell data specifically. Savitzky-Golay smoothing for noise, peak detection for max force, travel measurement, inflection point identification. All automated, runs on every test cycle.

I also built the monitoring platform with a data acquisition backend — sensors and equipment send data via REST APIs, stored in SQL, with scheduled analysis for failure trending and downtime calculations. The temperature monitoring uses thermocouples sampling every five seconds with offline buffering.

The framework handles high-frequency sampling, noise filtering, and automated anomaly detection. It's not academic — it runs in production daily.
microsoft.5:
When I joined Safran, everything was manual. Testing, tracking, reporting. Culture was 'we've always done it this way.'

[ACTION] I didn't come in swinging. Started small — built the robot arm as a proof of concept alongside the existing process. Nobody had to change anything while I proved it worked. Once technicians saw 30 minutes drop to 10, they became advocates.

Did the same with monitoring — built the platform, showed operations the downtime data they'd been missing, let results sell it.

[RESULT] Within months, the team was coming to me: 'can you automate this too?' Change management isn't convincing with arguments — it's showing results at small scale and letting success create demand.
microsoft.6:
Yeah, so we started having intermittent test failures — panels randomly failing that shouldn't have. Everyone wanted to recalibrate.

[ACTION] Instead, I pulled two weeks of force data into Python, analyzed every cycle. Found failures spiked in the afternoon. Cross-referenced with temperature logs — 3D-printed fixture was thermally expanding.

[RESULT] Root cause was material choice, not calibration. Switched materials, false failures dropped from 15% to under 1%. I approached it systematically — data first, hypothesis second, solution third. Not the other way around.
microsoft.7:
At Safran I needed a monitoring platform and looked at Grafana first — seemed obvious. But we needed custom business logic Grafana couldn't do: work order tracking, oven cure time validation, cross-referencing failures with production batches.

[ACTION] Built it from scratch. Python FastAPI backend, SQL database, REST APIs, web frontend. Equipment sends data via API calls, backend validates and stores, runs scheduled analysis, triggers automated notifications on thresholds.

[RESULT] Unplanned downtime: three days per month down to 30 minutes every six months. Not better equipment — better visibility. Automated alerts were the game-changer. Operations didn't have to watch dashboards — the system came to them.
microsoft.8:
Yeah, this relates directly to the Senior Test Engineer role. Microsoft wants someone who can test industrial control systems — that's literally what I do.

[SITUATION] At Safran I'm the person responsible for designing, building, and validating the entire test infrastructure. My robot arm system is an industrial control system — robot controller, sensors, embedded firmware, software, all integrated.

[ACTION] I've written formal test plans, test procedures, and test reports for system qualification. I've created data acquisition pipelines for sensor data collection. I've built the monitoring backend with APIs and databases. And I've documented everything — seven-plus service documents.

[RESULT] The experience maps directly: control system testing, sensor data acquisition, back-end software, test documentation. The domain shifts from aerospace panels to datacenter infrastructure, but the engineering discipline is identical.
microsoft.9:
I try to understand what 'customer' means broadly. My direct customers at Safran are the technicians who use my test system and the operations team who use my monitoring platform.

[ACTION] Early on I made the mistake of building what I thought was cool — a nice dashboard. Operations told me they didn't need another screen to watch. They needed automated alerts.

So I rebuilt it around their actual workflow. Now the system emails them when something needs attention. They don't have to check anything — it comes to them.

[RESULT] Usage went from 'nobody looks at it' to 'everyone relies on it.' Reduced unplanned downtime 99%. The lesson: customer impact isn't about building impressive technology. It's about solving the problem they actually have.
microsoft.10:
At Safran, I was juggling the robot arm, monitoring platform, Work Order Tracker, and five interns — all at once.

[ACTION] I prioritize by production impact. If the robot arm is down, production stops — that's priority one. Features and improvements get scheduled around production needs. For the interns, I time-boxed mentoring to three structured meetings per week so it didn't eat my whole day.

When stakeholders want competing things, I show them the data — 'fixing this robot issue saves X hours this week, your feature request saves Y hours next month. Which first?' That usually resolves it.

[RESULT] Everything shipped, just sequenced by impact. Transparency about trade-offs is key — don't say 'I'm busy,' show what each project is worth.
microsoft.11:
Growth mindset is basically 'I can't do this yet' instead of 'I can't do this.' Treating challenges as learning opportunities, not proof you're not good enough.

[EXAMPLE] When I needed STM32 firmware, I'd never written embedded C. Easy path: 'not my skillset, hire someone.' Instead I gave myself a week, worked through tutorials, and had production firmware by Friday.

But it's also about being open to being wrong. When operations said my monitoring dashboard was useless, I didn't get defensive. Asked 'what would actually help?' They said alerts. So I rebuilt it. That version reduced downtime 99%.

Growth mindset isn't just learning new tech — it's genuinely being open to hearing you're wrong.
microsoft.12:
My approach is writing docs that make the next person self-sufficient. If I get hit by a bus, can someone else run everything?

[ACTION] For the robot arm I wrote seven-plus documents: setup procedures, calibration guides, troubleshooting decision trees, known failure modes, maintenance schedules. Each targets a specific audience — technicians get step-by-step with photos, engineers get architecture and theory.

[RESULT] When the five interns started, they were operating the system independently within a week using just the documentation. That's the real test — not whether the docs exist, but whether a new person can actually use them.
microsoft.13:
[FOR SENIOR TEST ENGINEER — CO+I CTO OFFICE] CO+I powers Azure, Teams, Copilot — and the CTO Office tests next-gen industrial control systems for those datacenters. That's basically what I do now in aerospace. I built a control system with sensors, data acquisition, automated testing, and monitoring. The difference is scale — 100-plus datacenters, a million servers. I want my test engineering experience applied where reliability requirements are even higher and impact reaches a billion users. Plus I already have the back-end stack — Python, APIs, SQL, custom monitoring. Direct fit.

[FOR SENIOR MECH ENGINEER — DCE] Designing mechanical systems at hyperscale excites me. At Safran and Jenton I designed equipment from concept through commissioning — CAD, specs, vendor coordination, factory acceptance testing. Microsoft DCE does the same for cooling systems and infrastructure at a completely different scale. My Space Systems MSc also included thermal management and reliability in extreme environments — maps directly to datacenter challenges.
microsoft.14:
Microsoft specifically because of two things. First, the growth mindset culture is real, not just a poster on the wall. I've experienced it myself — every time I learned something new to solve a problem at Safran, that was growth mindset in action, I just didn't have a name for it. Microsoft has built an entire company culture around that principle and I want to be somewhere that values learning as much as delivering.

Second, the scale and impact. CO+I operates infrastructure that a billion people depend on. My monitoring platform at Safran serves maybe 50 people. I want to apply the same engineering discipline — data-driven, reliability-focused, automation-first — at a scale where it actually matters globally. And I live in Redmond, so honestly, it just makes sense.

amazon.0:
My official title at Safran is Automation Engineer — hired to build the robot arm test system. But a few months in I noticed equipment downtime tracking was completely manual. Notebook on a desk. Nobody analyzing patterns.

[ACTION] Nobody asked me to fix this. I just saw it and thought — we're losing three days a month of production and don't even know why. Built a full monitoring platform from scratch: Python backend, SQL database, REST APIs, web dashboard, automated alerts.

[RESULT] Unplanned downtime: three days per month down to 30 minutes every six months. Not better equipment — better visibility. I did this without being asked because the gap was obvious and the cost was clear. That's ownership.
amazon.1:
Okay so testing at Safran used to be: technician takes a panel, presses every switch by hand, checks force and continuity manually, writes everything down. Thirty minutes. Error-prone. Waste of skilled labor.

[ACTION] I simplified it to: robot presses, sensor measures, controller checks, software logs. Ten minutes, consistent, no human error.

But the real simplification was fixturing. Everyone assumed you'd need a custom fixture per panel type. I designed modular adapters snapping onto a universal base plate. One base, small swappable adapters. Killed 80% of the complexity.

[RESULT] 67% time reduction, 1,300-plus hours freed yearly, 75 panels per week. And because everything's logged digitally, we actually have traceable test data now — no more handwritten notes nobody can read.
amazon.2:
Had to choose between UFactory 850 and a Fanuc cobot. Fanuc was the 'safe' choice — bigger brand, better docs — but $15K more and overkill.

[SITUATION] Three days to decide before budget cycle closed.

[ACTION] Chose UFactory for three reasons: force requirements well within spec, Python SDK meant faster development, and the $15K savings funded the FUTEK sensors and STM32 hardware we needed regardless. Accepted less community support because our application was straightforward — pick-and-press, not complex path planning.

[RESULT] System's been running reliably. I documented every integration quirk for the next person. Decision worked because I matched tool to actual requirements, not to what felt safest.
amazon.3:
Robot arm started flagging good panels as failures. Technicians wanted me to recalibrate.

[ACTION] Didn't recalibrate blindly. Pulled two weeks of raw data into Python — every press cycle. SciPy analysis: smoothing, peak detection, statistics. Plotted force curves by pass/fail, time of day, panel type, fixture position.

Clear pattern — failures spiked in the afternoon. Cross-referenced temperature logs. 3D-printed fixture was thermally expanding — afternoon sun heats that side of the shop floor, shifting alignment 0.3mm.

[RESULT] Material choice, not calibration. Switched to low-thermal-expansion material. 15% false failures down to under 1%. Without diving into the data, we'd still be recalibrating weekly.
amazon.4:
When I started, test procedures were informal. Each technician had their own way. Inconsistent, undocumented, not traceable.

[ACTION] Created the standard. Formal test plans with explicit pass/fail criteria, documented procedures, calibration schedules, monitoring tracking every result. Seven-plus service documents — setup, calibration, troubleshooting, known failure modes.

This wasn't just about my system. I set the expectation that all test equipment should have this level of documentation.

[RESULT] When the five interns started, docs let them run the system independently within a week. Any engineer can pick up where I left off because the standards are documented, not in my head. Quality became the default.
amazon.5:
My manager didn't want to build the robot arm. Legacy equipment was 'fine.'

[ACTION] Didn't just accept it. Built a formal business case — PowerPoint, Excel ROI, real production data. Three numbers: $750-plus sensor replacements quarterly from crashes, three days monthly downtime, projected savings of 1,300-plus hours per year.

Also showed the robot could automate electrical continuity testing — technicians were doing that manually with test escapes happening. Quality risk, not just speed.

[RESULT] He approved it. Six months in production, projections holding. He's genuinely supportive now. I disagreed with data, not emotion — then committed 100% to making it succeed.
amazon.6:
At Safran, the five interns I supervised — I structured it so they'd do real engineering, not busy work.

[ACTION] Set up two-week sprints with clear milestones. Gave each person ownership of a defined scope. Met three times a week — coached them through problems rather than solving for them. Matched projects to skills: stronger coders got the ML model, the mechanical person did camera fixtures.

[RESULT] They delivered a 92% accuracy defect detection model and a working hardware prototype. One intern's work became the foundation for the vision gantry project. Two of them told me it was the most impactful internship they'd had because they shipped something real.
amazon.7:
We have this Work Order Tracker at Safran — 80-plus Arduino devices with touchscreens and barcode scanners across the building. I made the mistake of not planning for delivery timelines.

[SITUATION] First version — launched all features at once, overwhelmed everyone. People forgot to scan. Data was worthless.

[ACTION] Stripped it to bare minimum: scan in, scan out, show your rack. Got that working and adopted. Then slowly added features over months — labor time estimates, priority sorting, email notifications. Each feature only after people were comfortable with the previous one.

[RESULT] System's been stable over a year now. Every department uses it independently. Supervisors enforce scanning because they love the workload visibility. The lesson: tight deadline doesn't mean ship everything — it means ship the right thing first and iterate.
amazon.8:
Full architecture walkthrough.

[PROBLEM] Manual switch testing — 30 min/panel, technician shortage, inconsistent results, legacy equipment dying.

[COMPONENTS] UFactory 850 arm — 6-DOF, 5kg payload, Python SDK. FUTEK load cell — sub-Newton resolution. STM32 microcontroller — custom PCB with multiplexed GPIO for continuity testing.

[MAKE VS BUY] Bought robot and sensors — commodity items where vendor expertise matters. Built fixtures, firmware, and software — custom to our geometry and needed integration with monitoring platform.

[PROTOTYPING] Four-plus SolidWorks revisions, FDM to SLA printing. Each tightened alignment until sub-millimeter repeatability.

[VALIDATION] Tested with known-good and known-bad panels. Compared to manual testing for 100 panels — 99-plus percent agreement.

[DEPLOYMENT] Trained three technicians, wrote seven service docs, connected to monitoring platform.
amazon.9:
When selecting the force sensor, I evaluated three vendors. FUTEK claimed sub-Newton accuracy and millisecond response.

[ACTION] Didn't trust the spec sheet. Ordered eval units from FUTEK and a competitor. Designed a comparison: calibrated masses at various rates, checking linearity, repeatability, temperature drift.

FUTEK matched their claims. Competitor showed three times more drift at elevated temperatures — and our shop floor has real temperature swings.

[RESULT] Data justified FUTEK's 40% price premium. Negotiated volume pricing and spares agreement. Did similar validation for the UFactory arm — tested repeatability at our press forces, verified the SDK, stress-tested 10,000 cycles before production commitment. I don't deploy anything I haven't independently validated.
amazon.10:
When I built the monitoring platform, I had to make it scalable without overengineering.

[ACTION] Thought about it in terms of what Amazon calls 'think big' — I designed the database schema and API structure to handle not just the robot arm data, but any equipment in the building. Same platform tracks oven temperatures, work orders, failure modes, and sends different alert types to different people.

I also applied frugality — used open-source tools everywhere. Python, SQLite initially then SQL, no paid licenses. The whole platform cost basically nothing except my time.

[RESULT] The system scaled from monitoring one robot arm to tracking the entire manufacturing floor's equipment health. Built once, used everywhere. That one-to-many design was intentional from day one.
amazon.11:
When I needed the STM32 firmware for the continuity tester, I'd never written embedded C. Could have said 'hire someone' — would've taken weeks to find and onboard them.

[ACTION] Instead I learned it myself in a week. STM32CubeIDE tutorials, example projects, trial and error. Focused narrowly on what I actually needed: serial comms and GPIO reads. Didn't try to learn all of embedded programming.

[RESULT] Production firmware running on every test cycle. What I'm curious about next is exploring RTOS for more complex control sequences, and I've been reading about FreeRTOS integration with STM32. Learning doesn't stop when the project ships.
amazon.12:
The technicians at Safran weren't excited about the robot arm. Not 'it'll replace me' worried — more 'great, another complicated thing I have to learn.'

[ACTION] Three things. Made the UI simpler than the legacy equipment — fewer steps, clearer prompts. Stayed on the floor with them for several days across multiple sessions, answering questions in real time, adjusting the interface based on how they actually used it. And showed them the practical win — work orders come in batches of five, robot tests one at a time, so they can prep the next panel while the test runs.

[RESULT] Two weeks in, they went from 'I don't want this' to using it voluntarily. The turning point was feeling the productivity gain themselves, not me telling them about it. Trust came from demonstrated value, not promises.
amazon.13:
[FOR HARDWARE DEV ENGINEER — WWGS ROBOTICS] Amazon's grocery robotics is doing exactly what I love — ASRS, autonomous mobile robots, robotic arms — at a scale I've never worked at. At Safran I took a system concept to production: component selection, make-vs-buy, CAD, prototyping, vendor eval, test plans, deployment. Same work, but Amazon deploys across the entire grocery network.

Ownership culture fits how I work — monitoring platform and Work Order Tracker both exist because I saw gaps, not because anyone asked. JD's focus on prototyping, trade studies, and vendor evaluation is literally my daily work.
amazon.14:
Amazon specifically because of the Leadership Principles — they're not just corporate values, they're an actual operating system. Ownership, Dive Deep, Bias for Action — those aren't aspirational for me, they're descriptive. I built the monitoring platform because I owned the problem. I dove into force data to find the thermal expansion root cause. I shipped an 80% robot system in six weeks instead of waiting for perfection.

Also, grocery robotics is fascinating. The complexity of automating fulfillment at Amazon's scale — different products, different environments, massive throughput — that's a harder version of the problems I solve now. And I want hard problems.
